frameworks.name: Lateral Category Analysis
frameworks.definition: A problem-solving approach that recognizes most complex problems have already been solved in different domains, requiring transfer of solutions across category boundaries.

frameworks.name: TRIZ Problem Definition Linkage
frameworks.definition: A framework for identifying how poor problem definitions create false constraints that prevent recognition of solutions already existing in other domains.

claims.text: Most complex problems have already been solved somewhere, but in a different domain from where you are trying to solve them, and people struggle to make the linkage across domains (Rory Sutherland, TRIZ framework).
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: TRIZ identified that problem solutions cluster by physical constraint patterns rather than by industry or domain, making solutions transferable when the underlying constraint is recognized.
bounded_by: Requires recognizing structural similarity between different domains
Difficulty in cross-domain pattern transfer is primarily cognitive, not mechanical

claims.text: Poor problem definition creates false constraints: defining train overcrowding as 'anyone standing' masks the distinction between intermittent (100 people standing 10% of the time) versus continuous crowding (10 people standing 100% of the time), which are different problems requiring different solutions.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
claims.mechanism: Imprecise definitions conflate multiple constraint patterns into a single label, hiding the real constraint and preventing solution transfer from domains where that constraint has been solved.
bounded_by: Example uses train/tube transit; principle applies to any variable-demand system

claims.text: Definition quality determines solution search space: when a problem is defined incorrectly, the solver looks in the wrong category for solutions and misses solutions already deployed in other domains where the actual constraint is visible.
claims.speaker: Rory Sutherland
conditions: Complex problems with cross-domain solution precedents
bounded_by: Requires domain expertise or external perspective to recognize hidden constraints

examples.text: Train overcrowding in UK transit is commonly defined as 'anyone standing on a train.' Rory Sutherland notes this definition misses the real constraint: time-distribution of demand. A passenger standing for a short trip (10% of their commute) experiences minimal discomfort; a passenger forced to stand for 90% of their journey has a different problem. This hidden distinction prevents transit planners from recognizing that solutions from variable-demand industries (e.g., retail shelf-stocking systems, theatrical staging for intermittent crowds) could solve the *actual* problem but not the *defined* problem.

practices.text: When facing a complex problem, ask: has this constraint been solved elsewhere? Search adjacent industries and domains for precedents before inventing a new solution.

Interrogate your problem definition: does it distinguish between qualitatively different patterns that happen to look similar at first glance? Redefinition often reveals solutions already deployed in other fields.
